did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a deep learning approach for skin cancer classification. Specifically, it developed a MobileNetV3-based model enhanced with the Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) and optimized using Bayesian optimization. The model was trained and evaluated on the ISIC 2024 SLICE-3D dataset to classify benign, basal cell carcinoma, melanoma, and squamous cell carcinoma lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing a MobileNetV3-based model enhanced with CBAM for skin cancer classification. It details specific methodology including Bayesian optimization and data augmentation, reports new results on the ISIC 2024 SLICE-3D dataset (achieving 98.97% accuracy), and compares these findings against prior research, indicating it is an original study rather than a review.

title: Leveraging Convolutional Block Attention Module (Cbam) For Enhanced Performance In Mobilenetv3-Based Skin Cancer Classification
brief_summary: This study developed an automated multiclass skin cancer classification system by enhancing the lightweight MobileNetV3 architecture with the Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM). The model was trained and evaluated on the ISIC 2024 SLICE-3D dataset, which contains over 400,000 dermatoscopic images categorized into benign, basal cell carcinoma, melanoma, and squamous cell carcinoma classes. To address class imbalance and optimize performance, the researchers employed Bayesian optimization for hyperparameter tuning and applied targeted data augmentation techniques. The evaluation demonstrated that the proposed method achieved an accuracy of 98.97%, precision of 98.99%, recall of 98.97%, and an F1-score of 98.98%. These results surpassed previous state-of-the-art models by 1.86–6.52% while maintaining minimal additional computational demands, highlighting the effectiveness of combining lightweight CNNs with attention mechanisms for low-resource settings.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the four diagnostic categories in the ISIC 2024 SLICE-3D dataset used for multiclass classification. The model achieved 85.72% precision, 96% recall, and 90.56% F1-score for this class.
One of the four diagnostic categories in the ISIC 2024 SLICE-3D dataset used for multiclass classification. The model achieved 100% precision, 100% recall, and 100% F1-score for this class.

One of the four diagnostic categories in the ISIC 2024 SLICE-3D dataset used for multiclass classification. The model achieved 78.26% precision, 75.17% recall, and 76.59% F1-score for this class. The paper notes underperformance relative to other categories due to limited subset size and visual heterogeneity.

One of the four diagnostic categories in the ISIC 2024 SLICE-3D dataset used for multiclass classification. The dataset contained 400,552 benign samples out of 400,945 total samples. The model achieved 99.53% precision, 99.40% recall, and 99.46% F1-score for this class.

all_task_types.target: Diagnostic categories of skin lesions (Benign, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of skin cancer types using the ISIC 2024 SLICE-3D dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic labels (benign, BCC, melanoma, SCC) using a softmax activation layer to generate a probability distribution over these classes. Evaluation metrics include accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and partial Area Under the Curve (pAUC).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2024 SLICE-3D
datasets_used.description: Skin Lesion Image Crops Extracted from 3D TBP data from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2024 Competition. It contains de-identified image tiles extracted from 3D total-body photography at smartphone-comparable resolution, with detailed metadata including patient age, sex, anatomical site, lesion diameter estimates, and lighting modality. The dataset was collected from seven dermatologic centers globally.

use.training: The dataset was split into 70% for training.
use.validation: The dataset was split into 15% for validation.
use.testing: The dataset was split into 15% for testing.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Partial Area Under the ROC Curve (pAUC)

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Hyperparameter tuning to maximize model performance and explore the parameter space.

statistical_methods_used.method: Partial Area Under the ROC Curve (pAUC)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: Evaluation of classification performance, specifically focusing on high sensitivity regions (True Positive Rate > 80%) to minimize false negatives.
Assessment of classification performance by analyzing true positives, false positives, true negatives, and false negatives for each class.
Visualization and assessment of the trade-off between true positive rate and false positive rate across different thresholds.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, pAUC) and compared against prior research in a table, but no statistical significance testing was performed or reported.

ISIC 2024 SLICE-3D.notes: The proposed MobileNetV3-CBAM model was evaluated on the ISIC 2024 SLICE-3D dataset. The model achieved an overall accuracy of 98.97%. Class-specific performance varied, with SCC showing perfect classification (100% precision, recall, F1), while Melanoma showed lower performance (78.26% precision, 75.17% recall, 76.59% F1) due to limited subset size and visual heterogeneity. The confusion matrix analysis noted 24/25 BCC cases correctly identified, 1488/1500 benign cases correctly identified, 19/24 melanoma cases identified, and 11/11 SCC cases identified. The model outperformed prior state-of-the-art models by 1.86–6.52% in accuracy. Training took 26.1 hours. Bayesian optimization was used for hyperparameter tuning.

architecture_used.main_model: MobileNetV3Small enhanced with Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM)

architecture_details.attention_module: Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) with dual channel-and-spatial attention mechanisms
architecture_details.classifier_head: Dual dense layers with dimensions [512, 256] determined via Bayesian optimization

training_details.optimization_method: Bayesian optimization with Expected Improvement acquisition function

classification_or_output_head.classes: 4 classes (Benign, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma)

preprocessing_techniques: Dataset splitting: Stratified split into 70% training, 15% validation, and 15% testing subsets to eliminate data leakage.
Resizing: Uniform resizing of all images to 224 × 224 pixels.
Normalization: Pixel intensity normalization using ImageNet statistics to reduce internal covariate shifts.
Geometric augmentation: Random horizontal/vertical flips, ± 15° rotations, and ± 10 px translations.
Photometric augmentation: ± 20% brightness, ± 15% contrast, ± 10% saturation adjustments, and CLAHE (Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization).
Advanced augmentation: Motion blur with 3–5 px kernels, elastic deformations (α = 36, σ = 6), and coarse dropout of up to ten 16 × 16 px patches.
Noise removal: Specific removal of hair interference.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Random horizontal/vertical flips, ± 15° rotations, and ± 10 px translations.
± 20% brightness, ± 15% contrast, ± 10% saturation adjustments, and CLAHE (Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization).
Motion blur with 3–5 px kernels, elastic deformations (α = 36, σ = 6), and coarse dropout of up to ten 16 × 16 px patches.

conclusions: The proposed MobileNetV3-CBAM model achieved high classification performance on the ISIC 2024 SLICE-3D dataset, with an accuracy of 98.97%, precision of 98.99%, recall of 98.97%, and F1-score of 98.98%.

The integration of the Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) into the lightweight MobileNetV3 architecture effectively highlighted diagnostically relevant regions and improved feature extraction without significantly increasing computational demands or parameter counts.

The proposed method surpassed previous state-of-the-art models by 1.86–6.52% in accuracy, demonstrating the efficacy of combining lightweight CNNs with attention mechanisms and Bayesian hyperparameter optimization.
The model demonstrated strong clinical viability for low-resource settings due to its minimal computational overhead and rapid inference capabilities, despite the training time of 26.1 hours.

Class-specific performance varied, with Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) achieving perfect classification (100% precision, recall, F1-score), while Melanoma showed lower performance (78.26% precision, 75.17% recall, 76.59% F1-score) attributed to limited subset size and visual heterogeneity.

Bayesian optimization successfully identified optimal hyperparameters, including a learning rate of 2.26×10⁻⁴, dropout rate of 0.35, and L2 regularization strength of 2.5×10⁻⁵, leading to stable model convergence and effective regularization.

The study highlights the challenge of class imbalance in dermatological datasets, noting that while targeted data augmentation helped, the large disparity between benign (400,552 samples) and malignant lesion samples remains a factor influencing generalization for rarer classes.
important_remarks: The dataset exhibits extreme class imbalance, with 400,552 benign samples compared to very small malignant subsets (e.g., only 25 BCC and 24 Melanoma samples in the test set), which limits the generalizability of performance metrics for rare classes.

Melanoma classification performance was notably weak (78.26% precision, 75.17% recall) due to limited subset size and high visual heterogeneity, indicating potential failure modes for this critical cancer type.

Comparisons with prior state-of-the-art models are confounded by dataset mismatch; the proposed model was tested on ISIC 2024 SLICE-3D, while comparative baselines were evaluated on HAM10000 or ISIC-2019, making direct performance comparisons invalid.
The study lacks external validation on independent datasets, relying solely on a single internal split (70/15/15) of the ISIC 2024 SLICE-3D dataset.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of the reported performance improvements.
The reported AUC of 1.00 and pAUC of 0.2000 may be artificially inflated due to the small number of positive cases in the malignant classes (e.g., only 11 SCC cases in the test set).

The model was trained on image tiles extracted from 3D total-body photography at smartphone-comparable resolution, which may not fully represent the variability of standard clinical dermoscopic images.
Training time was substantial (26.1 hours), which may limit practical deployment in resource-constrained settings despite the model's lightweight inference architecture.
